turns.content: Imagine I have a game that needs to load the config file every time it starts, but I want to ensure it only reads from disk once per process run. My current approach uses an unsafe block to access a global variable directly, which my CI/CD pipeline flags as non-compliant. How can I safely achieve this singleton-like behavior without using unsafe code or manual memory management?

That is a very common hurdle when porting C-style state management into Rust. The core misconception here is that you must manually manage the lifecycle of your global data to prevent race conditions; in fact, the standard library and crates like once_cell provide safe abstractions specifically for this. Instead of using an unsafe block or raw pointers, you should leverage the OnceLock type from the std::sync namespace or the crate once_cell. These types are designed exactly for your scenario: they guarantee that initialization happens only once per thread and are automatically synchronized via mutexes internally if needed. You can wrap your config data in a OnceLock<Config> and call its .get() method whenever you need access, which will either return the cached value or initialize it safely on the first call.

I see, so I stop using unsafe blocks and just use OnceLock. But what if my application needs to start up very quickly and I don't want that tiny initialization delay? Is there a way to make this even more efficient for startup performance?

You are thinking about the right metric here: startup latency versus runtime correctness. For your specific case where you need zero-cost initialization on startup, LazyLock from once_cell is the superior choice over OnceLock because it employs lazy evaluation combined with thread-local storage. This pattern ensures that the expensive disk I/O or configuration parsing only occurs when the value is actually accessed by a thread, effectively making the first access free if no one has requested it yet. This is particularly useful for game engines where you want to minimize the cold-start time before the player can even see the menu. You would define your config as LazyLock<_, Config> and only call .get() inside your main loop or when a player attempts to load settings.

That makes sense, I'll switch to LazyLock for my global config. One last thing: does this pattern scale well if I have multiple modules all trying to access the same configuration data simultaneously?

Yes, it scales perfectly because LazyLock handles concurrency internally using a Mutex under the hood, but without you ever needing to write a sync::Mutex or handle lock ordering yourself. Multiple threads can safely call .get() on that single LazyLock instance concurrently; each thread will see the same cached result once initialized. You can also extend this pattern further by implementing traits like std::sync::OnceCell if you need slightly different initialization logic, but for most game configs, once_cell's LazyLock is the idiomatic and performant solution that your CI/CD pipeline will approve without flags.
